data.title: Lee Kuan Yew’s 38 Oxley Road home officially declared Singapore ‘national monument’
data.description:
Singapore’s government on Friday gazetted as a national monument the former home of founding prime minister Lee Kuan Yew, a site long at the centre of a bitter family dispute among his children.
The action marks a pivotal moment in the saga that has gripped the city state since Lee’s death in 2015, when his children publicly clashed over whether the bungalow at 38 Oxley Road should be preserved or torn down.
Lee’s oldest son, and former prime minister, Lee Hsien Loong, favoured preserving the property, which property agents in 2024 said had a prospective value of around S$30 million (US$23 million) in land-scarce Singapore.
But his two siblings – corporate executive Lee Hsien Yang and the late neurologist Lee Wei Ling – have pointed to language in their father’s will calling for its demolition.

The younger siblings accused their brother in 2017 of trying to exploit their father’s legacy for political gain, though the issue simmered down as Lee Wei Ling was still living on the property. She died last year.
On Friday, Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth David Neo issued a preservation order to gazette the site as a national monument, the ministry said in a statement.
The decision “took into consideration a written objection” by Lee Hsien Yang, who has been living in self-imposed exile abroad, citing political persecution which the government has denied.
The National Heritage Board had reviewed the objection, “and maintained its recommendation that the site is worthy of preservation as a National Monument for its historic significance and national importance,” the statement said.

It added that the decision was “made in the public interest, and goes beyond the personal preferences of any individual”.
The property was Lee’s home from the mid-1940s until his death. It was the site of significant events in the 1950s marking Singapore’s transition from a British colony to an independent nation.
It was also the venue for meetings of key leaders ahead of Singapore’s independence in 1965.

The government intends to acquire the site and undertake a detailed study to consider the next steps, including turning it into a heritage park, according to the statement.
The government would remove “all traces of” the Lee family’s private living spaces from the interior of the house and would not replicate them elsewhere, out of respect for his wishes to protect their privacy.
Lee is credited with transforming the Southeast Asian island into a wealthy city state in just a little over 30 years.

data.title: Hongkongers urged to get Covid vaccine as next infection wave approaches
data.description:
Hong Kong health authorities have urged the public to get the latest Covid vaccine as the next wave of the virus is set to arrive.
The Centre for Health Protection said on Friday active periods of Covid-19 came in cycles of every six to nine months.
More than five months had passed since the last periodic upsurge of Covid activity, it noted, as it asked the public to get the vaccine “in a timely manner” to reduce the risk of serious illness and death.
“According to the centre’s analysis, the active periods are associated with the changes in the predominant circulating strains and a decline in herd immunity in Hong Kong,” controller Dr Edwin Tsui Lok-kin said.
Since late March, the local predominant strains have changed from KP.2 and KP.3 to NB.1.8.1, according to the centre.

The latest surveillance data also indicates that NB 1.8.1 and LP 8.1 are currently the co-circulating strains in the local community.
“Currently, the approved Covid-19 vaccines are expected to remain effective against NB.1.8.1 and LP.8.1. There is no evidence suggesting that NB.1.8.1 or LP.8.1 would cause a more severe disease,” Tsui said.
“Nevertheless, the continuous evolving nature of the virus should not be taken lightly.”
Currently, the Hong Kong government is running a citywide Covid vaccination programme free of charge for eligible people.
Under the programme, those aged six months or above can receive free initial doses.
High-risk priority groups, including those aged 50 or above, pregnant women, healthcare workers and those aged 18 to 49 but with underlying comorbidities are eligible to receive booster doses for free at least six months after the last vaccination or Covid infection.
“As persons who contract influenza and Covid-19 at the same time are at a higher risk of severe complications and death, the [centre] strongly recommends that high-risk individuals receive seasonal influenza vaccination … and Covid-19 vaccination booster [shots] to reduce the risk of serious illness and death,” the centre said.
“The [World Health Organization] has also pointed out that high-risk individuals should receive booster doses at appropriate times to mitigate the risk of serious illness and death.”
According to official statistics, as of last month, about 93.8 per cent of the city’s population have had an initial vaccination against Covid-19. Some 71.6 per cent of elderly aged 80 or above and 79.2 per cent of those aged 65 to 79 have been covered.
The lowest coverage rate, at 49.7 per cent, was seen in the age group of six months to four years old.

data.title: WTT Finals Hong Kong: Wong Chun-ting, Doo Hoi-kem go close against Wang Chuqin, Sun Yingsha
data.description:
Hong Kong’s mixed doubles pair Wong Chun-ting and Doo Hoi-kem flirted with the biggest win of their careers at the WTT Finals Hong Kong before narrowly losing 3-2 to world and Olympic champions Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha on Friday night.
Playing in front of a packed Coliseum crowd – though the majority appeared to be supporting the visiting Chinese stars – Doo said she was playing “as if my life was on the line”, while Wong said he enjoyed “playing toe-to-toe” against the formidable Chinese pair.
“This was the best match we played at the Coliseum [over three nights],” Doo said after their 9-11, 11-5, 11-4, 1-11, 12-10 loss, which confirmed their elimination from the tournament. “It was better than I expected.
“There were a lot of extreme shots. I felt like I was playing with my life on the line, I had to dive on the ground to save many shots, and I think this experience will be something I can carry into every future game I play.”
Trailing 7-10 in the decider, the Hong Kong pair saved three match points to drag the game into deuce, and Wong recalled he was then thinking about how to score the next point.

“It was so close, but I do enjoy facing such formidable opponents,” he said. “We could manage [to win] two games again, and even forced them to deuce; I think it’s considered a breakthrough.
“There were skills that we hadn’t quite used before, but we were bold enough to try something new or integrate them into our foundations.”
Doo also heaped praise on her long-term playing partner, with whom she has won four World Championship bronze medals.
“Wong posted some significant threats in attacking them,” the 29-year-old said. “Especially in the fourth game, I felt as though I didn’t need to hit a ball in the first five points.
“It was just one shot and we won the point. He was that good and was carrying us a lot.”
Despite yet another loss and a now career 0-11 record against their familiar foes, Friday night was only the second time world No 5 Wong and Doo managed to get two games off Wang and Sun, who are ranked two places above them.
“It’s a pity to lose because all athletes want to win,” said the 34-year-old Wong. “Losing this match doesn’t mean we will always lose.
“This match gave us a confidence boost that we can still be competitive facing the top players. I hope we can qualify for this year-ending tournament by merit for the next two years. It won’t be easy but we will try our best.”
Wang and Sun, who were wild card entrants into the season finale, will now face Japan’s Sora Matsushima and Miwa Harimoto in Saturday’s semi-finals, while top seeds Lin Shidong and Kuai Man of China will play second-seeded South Koreans Lim Jong-hoon and Shin Yubin.
The final will also be held on Saturday, at 9pm.
Earlier, compatriot Chen Yi came back from behind to eliminate fellow Chinese Chen Xintong 4-1 in the women’s quarter-finals, walking away with a 7-11, 11-8, 11-9, 11-7, 11-2 win to set up another all-Chinese clash with reigning champion Wang Manyu.
In the men’s singles, world No 1 Wang sent Alexis Lebrun of France home with a commanding 11-6, 11-4, 11-9, 8-11, 11-4 quarter-final victory in 33 minutes.
More to come

data.title: Japan, US defence chiefs discuss radar row; Mexico’s China tariffs: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Japan, US defence chiefs cover radar row and other China tensions in phone call
US and Japanese defence chiefs spoke on the phone on Friday about a confrontation between Chinese and Japanese fighter jets over the weekend, with Tokyo saying it did not seek to escalate the tensions.
2. Mexico slaps tariffs of up to 50% on Chinese goods as US trade pact review looms
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum on Thursday defended her government’s decision to impose new import tariffs, hours after the Senate approved legislation allowing duties of up to 50 per cent on more than 1,400 products from Asian countries.
3. China urged to bring Japan’s Unit 731 atrocities to international court

In a new paper, archaeologists Wang Xiaohua and Xue Kaifan from the Heilongjiang Provincial Institute of Cultural Relics and Archaeology argue that Unit 731’s conduct meets the modern definition of crimes against humanity.
4. Trump, Modi have ‘warm’ phone call – but 2025 unlikely to end with a trade deal
Despite a high-level phone call between US President Donald Trump and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi on Thursday, 2025 is likely to end without the long-promised US-India trade deal, as the “America first” leader continues to stall the agreement while working to cement improved ties with Beijing.
5. Soft brain chip could help solve Neuralink’s big implant challenge

Scientists have developed a new type of semiconductor that they hope will overcome a major problem for bioelectronic devices: the immune response that causes the body to reject implants.
6. China to keep high fiscal deficit ratio in 2026 to buoy spending plans: analysts
China is expected to set its fiscal deficit ratio at around 4 per cent of gross domestic product in 2026, analysts said, adding the affirmation of a more prominent role for fiscal policy at this week’s central economic work conference indicates a selective approach to increasing government expenditures.
7. Why South Korea poses a major challenge to China’s Middle East arms export drive

Both neighbours hope to expand exports to the region, but the Koreans may be better placed due to systems integration and political concerns.

data.title: Ukraine says it ‘liberated’ two settlements in Kharkiv, encircling Russian troops
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky visited the strategic frontline town of Kupiansk on Friday, just as Ukrainian forces reported retaking parts of the railway hub and encircling Russian troops.
Standing at the city’s entrance in a bulletproof vest, Zelensky emphasised in a video message that “it is extremely important to achieve results on the front lines so that Ukraine can achieve results in diplomacy”.
“Many Russians talked about Kupiansk – we can see. I was here, I congratulated the guys. Thanks to every unit, to everyone fighting here, to everyone destroying the occupier,” he said.
The Khartiia Corps announced on social media that Kindrashivka, Radkivka, and several neighbourhoods in northern Kupiansk had been “liberated”.
“Today, we can say that the Russians in the city are completely cut off. For a long time, they couldn’t understand what was happening. But now they know they are surrounded,” Ihor Obolienskyi, commander of the Khartiia Corps of the National Guard, was quoted by the Ukrainska Pravda news outlet.
The Russian army claimed at the end of November to have again captured the city of Kupiansk, which it first seized in 2022 before Ukrainian forces retook it in the fall that year.
Ukraine denied the city had changed hands and fighting continued there.
The battlefield reports could not be independently verified.
Russia has in recent months touted advances on the battlefield, where Ukrainian forces are on the back foot.

Kyiv has repeatedly dismissed Russian claims of sweeping advances as attempts from Moscow to promote a narrative that Ukraine faces imminent collapse, to influence negotiations to end the war.
Washington is now pushing Kyiv to make major territorial concessions as part of its plan to end the nearly four-year war.
“Russians operate much more effectively in the information space than any of our partners and spread information that doesn’t reflect reality,” Zelensky told reporters on Thursday.
“We have to refute outright nonsense,” he added.
Russia has not claimed to have formally annexed the Kharkiv region, where Kupiansk is located, and according to Zelensky, the latest version of a draft US plan to end the war could see Russia withdraw from these areas, with Ukraine pulling out of the Donetsk region.

With US-backed peace efforts under way, Moscow has said it is advancing on all fronts. Moscow says its troops have also seized the strategic city of Pokrovsk in the east but Kyiv has denied this, saying fighting continues.
On Thursday, Russia said it had captured the eastern town of Siversk. Kyiv says it is under Ukrainian control.
Ukraine’s Deep State battlefield mapping project now shows at least three villages to the north and west of Kupiansk under Ukraine’s control.
Kupiansk’s northern districts are shown as also under Ukrainian control and the map suggests Russian troops are encircled in the city centre.

data.title: ‘Win without fight’: how China fast-tracked the J-36, left US years behind in sixth-gen jet race
data.description:
When it comes to building next-generation stealth jets, experience is the foundation of speed, reliability and combat readiness. Handing this critical job to a company with no stealth fighter experience could significantly delay progress and hand competitors a strategic gift.
This is an open message from Yang Shuifeng, a lead designer of China’s cutting-edge stealth fighters.
“The experience and capability of the research and development team cannot start from zero,” Yang, senior engineer and director of the performance research division at the Chengdu Aircraft Design Institute’s overall design department, wrote in a paper published in the peer-reviewed Journal of Systems Engineering and Electronics on November 28.
Titled “Aviation equipment R&D management method based on rapid capability generation”, the paper offers the first public glimpse of how China fast-tracks the development of J-36s and leaves the US years behind in the sixth-generation fighter race.
According to Yang and his co-authors, institutions with established “design capability, development infrastructure and production facilities” and deep familiarity with foundational industrial systems – such as relevant chips, materials, components and standard parts – can provide the scientific planning, process management, performance evaluation and oversight needed to “rapidly mobilise human, material and financial resources” and drive programmes forward at speed.
China and the US are the two leading countries developing sixth-generation fighters.
In March, US President Donald Trump awarded the US Air Force’s Next Generation Air Dominance (NGAD) X-47 contract to Boeing.
While Boeing’s rival Northrop Grumman has long been notorious for severe cost overruns and delays, Boeing itself has never designed or built an operational stealth fighter for the US military – its experience and capabilities in this domain are, in the eyes of many industry experts, nearly nonexistent.
The Post has contacted Boeing for comment.


Chengdu Aircraft Industry (Group) Company Limited (CAC), known as “Chengfei”, is the designer and manufacturer of the world’s biggest supersonic heavy stealth fighter fleet, the J-20.
By the end of 2024, its sixth-generation heavy stealth fighter – widely known as J-36 – was spotted undergoing intensive flight tests by the People’s Liberation Army Air Force, with rapid upgrades and iterations seen in recent months.
Meanwhile, Shenyang Aircraft Corporation (SAC), manufacturer of the J-35 stealth fighter, has been conducting extensive flight testing on its own sixth-generation lightweight stealth fighter, the J-50.
In contrast, US sixth-generation fighter development has progressed slowly. The NGAD programme, launched in 2014, remains largely on paper. The US military hopes Boeing can deliver a first flight by 2028.
US media reports attribute NGAD’s sluggish progress not only to technical challenges and the decline of American manufacturing but also to a series of management failures – persistent budget overruns, an inability to adapt to emerging military technologies and underestimating rivals, especially China. A too ambitious “do-everything-perfectly” mindset has also led to a plane that does too little well.
Yang’s team, in their paper, explain how China has avoided these management pitfalls.
When faced with budget overruns, Chinese engineers and scientists strive to revise aircraft designs and streamline R&D and manufacturing processes to reduce total costs rather than shifting the financial burden onto taxpayers.
More importantly, at the end of each year, project teams conduct a thorough review and “clear” any overruns, ensuring problems are not carried forward into the next fiscal cycle. These measures have prevented the kind of compounding, snowballing cost overruns seen across the Pacific.

For technological uncertainties, the Chinese team adopted a dual approach: they prepared for worst-case scenarios by setting high-performance benchmarks aimed at future warfare, while also staying grounded in reality – deliberately dropping secondary requirements to achieve breakthroughs in core capabilities.
Additionally, they maintained close coordination with China’s fast-evolving and dynamic manufacturing sector, actively embraced new technologies and integrated the latest innovations into weapon systems.
Yang likens the stealth fighter race to aerial combat between pilots. Each cycle consisted of detecting the target, processing information, making decisions and executing attacks. The side that completed this loop faster can launch more attacks within the same time frame and defeat its opponent.
To the Chinese, the idea of a programme like NGAD lingering in design debates for a decade is pointless.
“Equipment is meant to be used,” Yang and his team wrote.
Once formally delivered to users, the aircraft’s combat capabilities can be rapidly verified, applied and used in real-world operational scenarios. Post-delivery trials also allow developers to receive direct user feedback, enabling further optimisation and improvement of the aircraft.
“Once R&D, production and verification are complete, the aircraft should be quickly delivered to users and put into service,” Yang wrote. “If it remains sealed within the development unit for too long, it hampers functional refinement, performance enhancement and requirement fulfilment.”

Optimisation in aircraft development is endless, according to the Chinese engineers.
Passive optimisation comes from test results and field use – identifying issues that required modifications, upgrades or even software updates and hardware replacements within certain production batches.
Active optimisation arises from new R&D demands, evolving combat doctrines, and emerging aerospace technologies – allowing aircraft to reach new states and acquire new capabilities.
“After 10 or even dozens of iterative improvements, the platform, systems, propulsion and armaments of the first and last variants may differ greatly – but their performance and combat effectiveness could see generational leaps,” Yang wrote.
According to a report by China Aviation News in June 2020, Yang travelled to Beijing during pandemic lockdowns to make a significant contribution to securing national approval for new fighter programmes.
“Rapid development aims to deliver combat capability swiftly,” Yang wrote. “In military competition and preparation, it allows us to make the first move, seize the initiative and possibly achieve victory without fighting.”
But, he adds: “This necessarily breaks existing R&D systems and processes – it represents a fundamental reshaping of traditional equipment development models.”

data.title: Would the world be safer if the military were in charge of the US?
data.description:
Tom Barrack, the United States special envoy for Syria, recently made waves at an international forum in Doha, Qatar, by suggesting that “benevolent monarchies” might be the most effective form of government for the Middle East. He also suggested that Israel was not a democracy.
His remarks were probably welcomed in some parts of the region, but probably less well received by the US and other Western media, which all but consider themselves guardians of proper world opinion.
Barrack was just stating the political reality on the ground. Remember the last time the US professed to bring democracy to the region – by invading Iraq and overthrowing Muammar Gaddafi in Libya? Well, how did all that turn out?
But that also had me thinking about a military government in the US.
Would the world be a safer place if Pentagon generals and admirals were in charge in Washington, rather than someone as capricious as President Donald Trump and as clueless as his defence secretary Pete Hegseth, a former Fox News TV co-host?
I admit that it would not be good for American “democracy”, such as it is – or would it?

However, I am not American, but part of the rest of the world. If you are a fisherman living in the Caribbean at the moment, or a resident in places such as Ukraine and Venezuela, wouldn’t you be safer with the more cautious Pentagon than with the impulsive White House?
As for the rest of the world, wouldn’t it be safer if the so-called nuclear football – the device that reportedly follows the US president wherever he goes, for him to launch a nuclear war – were instead placed in the care of, say, the joint chiefs of staff, who would know far more than the “commander-in-chief” about its irreversible global consequences?
I was just listening to a podcast, Inside Trump’s Head, affiliated with the news outlet The Daily Beast and co-hosted by Michael Wolff, author of four books on Trump. He was particularly insightful about the interaction of the American civilian leadership and military services, and the contemporary reversal of the trigger-happy generals and responsible civilian leaders.
“The context here is kind of elemental, which is that we have always considered that the military would be run by men of a certain disposition and point of view who needed to be restrained,” he said.

“Essentially, they were warriors, and there were all kinds of reasons not to go to war … if you saw the grander picture to restrain that instinct. You want people who can go to war if you need to go to war. But the last thing you want to do is always go to war. So, therefore, you had a civilian authority that was in charge of these kinds of people and that instinct to always pull the trigger.”
That was certainly the case when Harry Truman sacked the out-of-control Douglas MacArthur during the Korean war and when general Curtis LeMay, then chief of staff of the US Air Force, tried unsuccessfully to push John F. Kennedy into war during the Cuban Missile Crisis. Now, though, Wolff argues that military and civilian roles have seemingly been reversed.
“I think it has seldom been contemplated that that balance would be wholly turned around, that you would have the military as rules-bound experts, highly skilled in what they do [and] very knowledgeable about and attentive to the nuances of these kinds of very combustible situations … and [who are] being run by people who are just off the beam who have no comprehension of what’s at stake here, who are just the macho guys who want to do the macho war.”
Wolff was commenting on orders by Trump and Hegseth to strike alleged drug traffickers – without due process or any offer of evidence – at sea in the Caribbean, acts which experts claim are war crimes.

“These guys, Hegseth and Trump, are not engaging in the extremely complex combat decisions related to, for instance, Ukraine,” he said.
Wolff described the war in Ukraine as “a difficult situation, a situation which requires some attention of these attention-deficit guys”, as opposed to “an unarmed small boat, in the middle of the sea in which nothing is at stake other than the lives of some purported drug couriers – this is just shameful”.
Without romanticising US generals and admirals, I would wager that the current joint chiefs at the Pentagon are far more knowledgeable and adept at running the American security state and the military-industrial complex than the Trump administration.

data.title: Yuen Long cafe’s meat patties skyrocket to fame after viral online copypasta trend
data.description: Yuen Long Tai Bo Cafe shot to Internet fame after it became the subject of a viral copypasta trend on Threads.\nThe lengthy text, which has been copied and pasted on various online posts repeatedly, playfully referred to the cafe’s meat patties as “a tastebud nuke” and “Asia’s number one”, prompting a wave of customers to experience the hype, some from as far as Macau.\n

data.title: Chinese robotaxi firm Pony.ai bets on ‘asset-light’ strategy for growth
data.description:
Chinese autonomous-driving technology firm Pony.ai is betting on an “asset-light” strategy and newer generations of low-cost driverless cars to drive growth for its robotaxi operation as the company expects to break even by 2030.
Under the asset-light model, Pony.ai would team up with third-party companies – such as taxi operators or ride-hailing platforms – that would fund the deployment of its robotaxi fleet, according to Leo Wang Haojun, chief financial officer.
Instead of taking on the full weight of owning the fleets, the Guangzhou-based company would sell its driverless cars to third parties and license its autonomous-driving technology and fleet-management expertise for a fee, Wang said. It would also take a cut of fares, he added.
“We expect the asset-light model to enable more efficient [fleet] expansion for us,” Wang said on Thursday.
Last month, Pony.ai expanded its partnership with Chinese ride-hailing company Sunlight Mobility, which operates in more than 180 cities, to deploy an initial fleet of robotaxis in Guangzhou, the capital city of the southern province of Guangdong.
The fleet will use Pony.ai’s seventh-generation vehicles and is scheduled to launch by the end of the year, with plans to expand to more Chinese cities.
The company’s recent announcement that it had broken even in Guangzhou on a per-vehicle basis was a big draw for mobility operators to partner with the company, Wang said. The news validated the model and signalled the increasing sustainability of robotaxi commercialisation, he added.
Wang cited the rapidly decreasing costs of the company’s self-developed driverless cars as a key contributor to its robotaxi revenue growth.


Costs for its seventh-generation vehicles’ autonomous driving kit (ADK) – including lidar sensors, other sensors and additional components – declined 70 per cent from the previous generation, according to Wang.
“Our own estimate is that ADK cost will keep trending down,” he said. “So the money saved will turn into our margin.”
The company did not disclose the cost of its robotaxis, which are built using vehicles from domestic firms BAIC Group and GAC Group, as well as Japan’s Toyota.
Rival Baidu Apollo Go’s latest-generation robotaxi, the RT6, costs around US$28,000.
Pony.ai expects to roll out 1,000 vehicles by the end of this year and to triple the total next year. Wang expected the company to break even by 2030, when it would have around 100,000 vehicles deployed.
The company operates commercial fleets in Chinese cities including Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou and Shenzhen. It also has agreements with eight countries, including the US, Singapore, South Korea and the United Arab Emirates, to trial robotaxi operations.
US and Hong Kong-listed Pony.ai reported revenue of US$25.4 million for the quarter ended September, a 72 per cent increase from a year earlier. It cited robotaxi services and technology licensing for the increase. Its net loss widened around 40 per cent to US$61.6 million.
The company raised HK$6.7 billion (US$864 million) in its Hong Kong stock debut in November. Its shares have also been listed on the Nasdaq market since November 2024.

data.title: China to keep high fiscal deficit ratio in 2026 to buoy spending plans: analysts
data.description:
China is expected to set its fiscal deficit ratio at around 4 per cent of gross domestic product in 2026, analysts said, adding the affirmation of a more prominent role for fiscal policy at this week’s central economic work conference indicates a selective approach to increasing government expenditures.
In a statement issued after the annual conference ended on Tuesday, officials called for maintaining a “necessary deficit size, total debt and expenditure volume”. The December meeting of high-level policymakers typically sets the broad agenda for economic work in the coming year.
Analysts said the conference’s reiteration of the need for “proactive” fiscal policy means this year’s 4 per cent ratio – the highest in the country’s history – is likely to be extended into next year to keep pace with increases in government spending.
“The deficit ratio may be set at between 4 and 4.2 per cent, and the deficit total may increase from 5.66 trillion yuan (US$801.5 billion) in 2025 to 6 to 6.25 trillion yuan in 2026, an increase of nearly 600 billion yuan,” said Lian Ping, director general of the China Chief Economists Forum, in a note published on Friday.
“Overall GDP growth, which may reach 148.7 trillion yuan in 2026, has made bigger deficits possible, which will in turn provide a stable source of funds for expenditure and underpin expenditure increase from 2025’s base,” he added.
Raymond Yeung, chief economist for Greater China at ANZ, said along with the possible 4 per cent ratio target, the broad fiscal deficit is expected to reach 9 to 10 per cent of GDP in 2026, but stressed the most relevant feature would not be its size.
“The key is the imperative to ‘optimise the structure of fiscal expenditure’ mentioned in the readout. So Beijing is expected to place greater emphasis on the effectiveness and targeted nature of its policies – balancing whether funds will be used for areas such as debt resolution, infrastructure, technology or consumption,” Yeung said.
In an editorial published earlier this month in the People’s Daily – the press organ of the ruling Communist Party – China’s finance minister Lan Foan called for a more proactive fiscal policy to boost demand, strengthen industrial security and advance tech while mitigating debt risks. He also affirmed the need to maintain strict oversight of fiscal spending and local government borrowing.
There will be expectations that more fiscal expenditures will be spent on livelihood and welfare, as well as programmes that can boost consumption
Zhou Zheng, China Macro Group
Earlier this week, Lan also told state news agency Xinhua on the sidelines of the conference that the continuation of a more proactive fiscal policy was a “significant policy determination” intended to “cope with external shocks” as well as “internal risks and challenges”.
Lian of the China Chief Economists Forum, meanwhile, estimated a 200 billion yuan increase in the allocation of ultra-long-term “special” bonds in 2026, bringing the total to 1.5 trillion yuan to fund projects related to major strategic goals and building up security capacity. On top of this, Lian said, 500 billion yuan in special bonds could be allocated to stabilise the country’s ailing property sector and for repaying or restructuring debts.
Local governments, some of which have had trouble generating revenues, will have more relief in the form of a higher debt quota, likely 4.8 trillion yuan, for 2026, up from this year’s 4.4 trillion limit, Lian added.
Other observers said increases in expenditures from a higher deficit ratio must be used to benefit China’s people.
“Beijing is talking about boosting and optimising government spending. This is against the backdrop that consumers and non-state sectors remain timid, and it will take time to boost their spending,” said Zhou Zheng, a senior analyst with the Zurich-headquartered consultancy China Macro Group.
“There will be expectations that more fiscal expenditures will be spent on livelihood and welfare, as well as programmes that can boost consumption,” Zhou said.
“Beijing has underlined discipline and optimisation not to tread the old path of investing in sectors with excess capacity or fuelling ‘involution-like’ competition.”
While Beijing does not set specific macroeconomic targets at the central economic work conference – leaving those for the annual legislative sessions in March – the meeting usually provides a set of priorities for government bodies to follow.
At this year’s legislative meetings, China set its deficit ratio at about 4 per cent, up 1 percentage point from the previous year. It also unveiled 4.4 trillion yuan in local government special-purpose bonds.
